``SwaggerMappingError`` could be raised if (the list is not exhaustive but gives an idea about the implications)
 - the response spec is not specifying the type (which is weird but possible, especially if no response validation is defined) -> https://github.com/Yelp/bravado-core/blob/619dec76ecbb67858129b808e8af4e5f8496e7a6/bravado_core/marshal.py#L60
 - the response body is not conform to the response spec (attribute ``attr`` is specified to be of type ``object``, but the value to marshal has ``attr`` set to ``1``)  -> https://github.com/Yelp/bravado-core/blob/619dec76ecbb67858129b808e8af4e5f8496e7a6/bravado_core/marshal.py#L133
 - An ``attr`` is defined  as type ``string`` and format ``date`` but the view returned object has ``attr`` set to date string representation and not ``datetime.date`` instance -> https://github.com/Yelp/bravado-core/blob/619dec76ecbb67858129b808e8af4e5f8496e7a6/bravado_core/formatter.py#L43

The idea of acting as NO OP is because this cases should be already handled by the downstream renderer or the service is returning a not spec compliant response. (which is the current behaviour)

@erikbern, I don't understand the rational behind using the euclidean distance here?

---
To try to clarify this from an API standpoint:

* The distance function is exposed to the users as follows:
```py
t = AnnoyIndex(2, 'dot')
t.add_item(0, [0, 1])
t.add_item(1, [1, 1])
d = t.get_distance(0, 1)
```
where `get_distance` is defined as follows:
```cpp
T get_distance(S i, S j) {
    return D::normalized_distance(D::distance(_get(i), _get(j), _f));
  }
```

*  The doc does not specify anything special about the distance: "Returns the distance between items `i` and `j`."

* Therefore, I believe the user would expect that `t.get_distance(v_1, v_2)` would use the distance specified in `AnnoyIndex(f, {DIST})`.
So in this case it would be `dot(vectors[v_1], vectors[v_2])`. 

---

One issue is that `dot(x, y)` is more a similarity than a distance. I assume `-dot(...)` is more convenient than `C - dot(...)` since the dot is not normalized.

I guess in this case we could return either `-dot(original_vec_i, original_vec_j)` or `1 - cos(augmented_vec_i, augmented_vec_j)`.
(where `original_vec_i` is of dimension `N` and `augmented_vec_i` is of dimension `N+1`).

Given the API example above, returning the `dot` value seems to make more sense to me than returning the `cos` value.

---

I'm trying to work out what this does in the face of code that's already been fixed (i.e. is already using `-E`). I think this doubles the `-E` which isn't ideal.
```
In [1]: testA = r'''#!/usr/bin/env python
   ...: print 'hello'
   ...: '''

In [2]: testB = r'''#!/usr/bin/env python -E
   ...: print 'hello'
   ...: '''

In [3]: import re

In [4]: p = re.compile(r'\A' + '#!/usr/bin/env python')

In [5]: p.search(testA)
Out[5]: <_sre.SRE_Match at 0x7f19ea8acc60>

In [6]: p.sub('#!/usr/bin/env python -E', testA)
Out[6]: "#!/usr/bin/env python -E\nprint 'hello'\n"

In [7]: p.sub('#!/usr/bin/env python -E', testB)
Out[7]: "#!/usr/bin/env python -E -E\nprint 'hello'\n"
```

Letting it search to the end of the line looks like it works:

```
In [20]: p = re.compile(r'\A' + '#!/usr/bin/env python.*')

In [21]: p.sub('#!/usr/bin/env python -E', testA)
Out[21]: "#!/usr/bin/env python -E\nprint 'hello'\n"

In [22]: p.sub('#!/usr/bin/env python -E', testB)
Out[22]: "#!/usr/bin/env python -E\nprint 'hello'\n"
```

A few things here:

1. It would be slightly more efficient to just use `messages[token.name] = current.messages[token.name]` here rather than copying every k:v pair each time.
2. This breaks scoping rules somewhat. By mutating this global dictionary, it's now possible for outer scoped messages to use the child messages of their siblings. This approach also means you can't have two messages, `A` and `B`, that both have distinct children named `C`, even though that's perfectly legal according to formal protobuf spec.
3. Related to above, in the above example we should be able to reference `A.C` in message `B`.

That being said, I think this is a great start and gets us most of the way there.

The only thing required by a command module is the following lines of code:
```Python
    def module_block_extension(self, ext):
        if ext.name == 'myextension':
            return True
```

The advantage of a callable that returns True if the extension should be blocked is:
1. I don't have to right custom parsing logic to handle strings like `'azext_mycustomext2 <= 0.5.0'` and determine the semantic meaning of it. I didn't want to do this.
2. An optional 'reason' can be specified and added to the result so command authors can state why they are suppressing an extension.
3. While I didn't include it in my example, the extension object that gets passed to `module_block_extension()` has all the metadata of that extension. So you can suppress by more than just the name and version. e.g. You can filter by say [author](https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli-extensions/blob/master/src/index.json#L63) if you only want to block it if it was the extension authored by you.

Few thoughts on this section.

1. We don't actually display any information about how to install the certificates. Doing so is extremely webserver specific. We have a bit of that information under [where are my certificates](https://certbot.eff.org/docs/using.html#where-are-my-certificates).
2. `auth` doesn't install certificates, it simply obtains them.
